one_line_takeaway: ZEDD detects LLMail-Inject-style prompt injections by measuring cosine drift between a candidate prompt and an LLM-generated 'clean' rewrite of it, reaching ~95% accuracy with fine-tuned Llama-3/Mistral/Qwen2 encoders at a 2-6% clean false-positive rate, but Jailbreak and Task Override categories are detected noticeably less reliably than Encoding Manipulation or System Leak, and the paper does not test adaptive attackers designed to evade the embedding-drift signal itself.
key_finding: At the 3% clean-FPR cap operating point (Table 2), Llama-3 8B Instruct reached 95.32% accuracy / 95.85% precision / 94.75% recall / 95.30% F1 at 5.5% clean FPR; Mistral 7B Instruct reached 95.55% accuracy / 96.58% precision / 94.45% recall / 95.50% F1 at 2.3% clean FPR; Qwen2-7B Instruct reached 95.46% accuracy / 96.27% precision / 94.52% recall / 95.38% F1 at 2.2% clean FPR; Sentence-BERT (All-MPNET-Base-V2) trailed at 90.75% accuracy / 99.65% precision / 81.78% recall / 89.84% F1 at 1.7% clean FPR. Per-category flag rates (Table 1) show consistently weaker detection for Jailbreak (86.2%-92.2% across models) and Task Override (86.7%-90.8%) than for Encoding Manipulation (95.9%-98.2%) and System Leak (91.6%-96.9%), with Sentence-BERT the weakest encoder overall, especially on Jailbreak, Encoding Manipulation, and System Leak. Fine-tuning each of the four models took ~15-18 minutes on a single NVIDIA B200 GPU, and the authors report full evaluation over the ~51,000-pair test set completed in under 8 minutes. Ablations at 5% and 10% clean-FPR caps (Tables 5-6) show the flag rate on injected categories stays roughly flat while the clean-FPR itself rises toward the cap, i.e., loosening the cap mainly increases false alarms rather than materially increasing recall on injected prompts. The paper's headline claim that ZEDD 'outperforms existing models' is substantiated only via a qualitative appendix comparison (Figure 2) against numbers reported in a separately cited paper (Ayub & Majumdar, embedding-based prompt-injection classifiers), not via a re-run, controlled baseline comparison within the paper's own pipeline.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Zero-Shot Embedding Drift Detection (ZEDD), a lightweight detector for direct and indirect prompt injection attacks in LLM-integrated applications. ZEDD measures the cosine-distance ('drift') between the embedding of a candidate prompt and a matched 'clean' rewrite of that prompt, using an encoder that is fine-tuned once (on clean-clean and injected-clean pairs) and then applied zero-shot at inference. A two-component Gaussian Mixture Model (with a Kernel Density Estimation fallback) is fit over the resulting drift-score distribution to set a flagging threshold, with the threshold chosen via constrained binary search to hit a target clean false-positive-rate cap (tested at 3%, 5%, and 10%) while maximizing overall flag rate (soft target ~50%). The authors evaluate four encoders (Sentence-BERT All-MPNET-Base-V2, Llama-3 8B Instruct, Mistral 7B Instruct, Qwen2-7B Instruct) after fine-tuning each for roughly 15-18 minutes on an NVIDIA B200 GPU (RunPod), and report per-category flag rates and aggregate accuracy/precision/recall/F1 across the five injection categories they define (Jailbreak, System Leak, Task Override, Encoding Manipulation, Prompt Confusion).
threat_model: Attacker goal: inject adversarial instructions into email content processed by an LLM-integrated email assistant, mapped to five semantic manipulation patterns: (1) Jailbreak via role-play/hypothetical framing, (2) System leak to extract system prompts/config, (3) Task override to redirect the assistant to unauthorized actions, (4) Encoding manipulation using special characters/obfuscation to evade detection, (5) Prompt confusion via convoluted multi-step instructions. Attacker knowledge: public/inferable information about the target LLM-integrated application (email formatting conventions, general model behavior via documentation or trial interaction) and public prompt-injection techniques (e.g., from LLMail-Inject), but no access to private model weights or internal application architecture. Attacker capability: limited to crafting and sending malicious email content ingested by the assistant (classic indirect prompt injection via untrusted content channel), with iterative refinement based on observable system responses, but no control over model infrastructure. This is explicitly the LLMail-Inject threat model, restated by the authors for their own evaluation setting.
limitations: The authors state these themselves: (1) detection quality is tied entirely to the chosen embedding model's ability to capture semantic meaning, and different-sized/architected LLMs produce different embedding spaces, so drift quality may not transfer; (2) scalability to larger models/datasets is untested and may require adaptive resource-conservation methods; (3) the method is evaluated only on email-formatted content (LLMail-Inject), and the authors explicitly plan to test on other data formats in future work; (4) the lightweight design trades off robustness -- more sophisticated injected prompts may bypass ZEDD, and prompts could plausibly be crafted to manipulate the embedding-level drift signal directly (an adaptive-attacker scenario the paper does not empirically test); (5) the authors position ZEDD only as a 'strong first defense layer,' not a complete solution, and defer adaptive-attack robustness to future work. Additional grounding-level caveats not stated by the authors: the 'clean' counterfactual prompts used for computing drift are themselves LLM-generated (GPT-3.5-turbo) rewrites rather than naturally occurring benign prompts, so drift is measured relative to a synthetic rewrite rather than an independently sourced benign baseline; category labels (five injection types) were also assigned by GPT-3.5-turbo rather than human annotation, so category-level error patterns (e.g., weaker Jailbreak/Task Override detection) partially reflect the label generator's categorization boundaries; and the comparative 'outperforms existing methods' claim rests on an appendix figure comparing against externally reported numbers rather than a same-pipeline baseline re-implementation.

datasets_used: Microsoft LLMail-Inject dataset (461,640 initial injected entries; reduced via dedup/English-filtering/categorization/clean-pair generation to 171,999 usable entries)
Synthetically generated clean-clean prompt pairs (~86,000) via GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 / OpenAI Batch API, used as a similarity baseline alongside ~86,000 subsampled injected-clean pairs
Held-out test slice: 51,603 aligned pairs (25,801 clean-clean, 25,802 injected-clean)
quantitative_results: Llama-3 8B Instruct: 95.32% accuracy, 95.85% precision, 94.75% recall (adversarial), 95.30% F1, 5.5% clean FPR
Mistral 7B Instruct: 95.55% accuracy, 96.58% precision, 94.45% recall, 95.50% F1, 2.3% clean FPR
Qwen2-7B Instruct: 95.46% accuracy, 96.27% precision, 94.52% recall, 95.38% F1, 2.2% clean FPR
Sentence-BERT (All-MPNET-Base-V2): 90.75% accuracy, 99.65% precision, 81.78% recall, 89.84% F1, 1.7% clean FPR
Average clean FPR across all four models at 3% cap: 2.93%
Per-category flag rates range roughly 86.2%-98.2% depending on model/category (lowest on Jailbreak and Task Override, highest on Encoding Manipulation)
Fine-tuning time: ~15-18 minutes per model on a single NVIDIA B200 GPU (RunPod); full ~51,000-pair evaluation in under 8 minutes
baselines_compared: No same-pipeline reproduced baseline; comparison to prior work is via Appendix Figure 2, which juxtaposes ZEDD's results against numbers reported in Ayub & Majumdar's 'Embedding-based classifiers detect prompt injections and adversarial inputs' (cited as reference [12]/[20]), described as using Logistic Regression, XGBoost, and Random Forest classifiers over embeddings rather than fine-tuned drift scoring
